title: Duplication & Cannibalization Audit — All 7 URLs

duplicate_titles.detail: All 7 pages have unique titles. No title duplication detected.

duplicate_meta.detail: All meta descriptions are unique and page-specific.

duplicate_h1.detail: All H1s are unique. Each references specific use case and numbers.

repeated_body_blocks.detail: Zero shared template blocks. Each page has unique 'what this means', 'when it makes sense', 'when it's risky', comparison table, and FAQ content.

same_intent_overlap.detail: Each page targets a DIFFERENT search intent: emergency loan, good-credit benchmark, consolidation decision, auto/large purchase, fair-credit warning. No two pages target the same query intent.

vs_parent_loan_calculator.detail: Scenario pages target specific long-tail queries ('$5K at 15%'). Parent calculator targets head term ('loan calculator'). Different intent tiers. Scenario pages LINK to parent calculator — supporting, not competing.

vs_20k_loan_blog_post.detail: Scenario page serves CALCULATOR intent (user wants the payment number). Blog serves EXPLANATORY intent (user wants to understand loan mechanics). Google typically shows BOTH for informational queries — a calculator result AND an article. They complement, not compete.
vs_20k_loan_blog_post.mitigation: Cross-link them: scenario page → 'Read our detailed guide on $20K loans' linking to blog. Blog → 'Calculate your exact $20K loan payment' linking to scenario page. This signals Google they're different content types.

scenario_pages_vs_each_other.detail: No two pages target the same query. $20K at 8% (good credit) and $20K at 20% (fair credit) use the same amount but target DIFFERENT audiences and intents — one is aspirational (what you can get with good credit), the other is a warning (what you'll pay with fair credit).

summary: NO duplication detected. NO cannibalization risk. The 5 hand-crafted pages target distinct intents and audiences. Cross-linking plan strengthens rather than dilutes.
